raw_text: John Warden. " Ripae ulterioris amore: Structure and Desire in Aeneid 6." CJ95 (2000) 349-361. Smith argues that Vergil shaped the Sinon-Laocoon episode to be a story of the systematic perversion of religious sacrifice that turns inevitably on the perversion of human knowledge. Homer generally treated the last two feet of the epic verse as an unbroken rhythmical unit. Pontes shows that the Aeneid resorts frequently to sensebreaks, attested by ancient punctuation, within the heroic clausula. He considers the rhetorical and poetic effect of the 'broken' heroic clausula in Aeneid 2 and 3.. Casali tracks ambiguity and complexity in Dido's address to Aeneas {Aen. 4.416-36) and explores the implications of her request for tempus inane : did Aeneas make a mistake? Warden supplies a detailed discussion of correspondences 140 Alexander G. McKay and contrasts in passages that precede the cr relate to the crossing and what lies beyond.

Gomez Pallares links the basic meaning of latro (Aen. 12.1-8) (an armed man who stalks and wounds his victim) with the popular Roman board game (ludus latrunculorum ) where military strategy was basic. raw_text: S. C. Smith. "Remembering the enemy: narrative, focalization, and Vergil's portrait of Achilles." TAPA 129 (1999) 225-261. Fantham's study of Turnus' role in the council debate ( Aeneid 11) and in the battle episodes from his triumph in Aeneid 10 to his death, sets Turnus in a more compassionate light; Vergil's depiction of the resistance hero's weakness, and his absorption with an egocentric quest for glory, nevertheless retains our pity for the defeated hero. Papaioannou demonstrates that Vergil transformed the mentality and behaviour of Diomedes in Homeric epic and incorporated his transformation in the new intellectual milieu of the Augustan age. Diomedes has no enemy, and no rival in Patavium; his destiny foreshadows Aeneas' cultural integration. Smith accents Vergil's use of Achilles as nuanced figure, central to an understanding of the epic poem and its hero. Achilles, on the testimony of Aeneas, Priam,, and Neptune, emerges not simply as a symbol of violence but as a more complex figure embodying wrath and pietas. Just as Achilles shares Aeneas' pietas, so Aeneas shares Achilles' destructive nature. 142 Alexander G. McKay

raw_text: R. F. Thomas. Reading Virgil and His Texts ; Studies in Inter textuality. Ann Arbor 1999. Bell argues that Vergil's epic verses are as solicitous of popular favor as of learned connoisseurship. Vergil makes his poem speak directly in a " popularis " manner to all who had seen the political "advertisements." Gladiatorial imagery, which surfaces in the combat scenes, was familiar to all ranks of Roiman society. The late Don Paul Fowler leaves his collected articles as legacy; they are abiding memorials of a learned, provocative scholar and mentor. Gladiatorial imagery, which surfaces in the combat scenes, was familiar to all ranks of Roman society. Pinkster analyses Vergil's use of the present tense for narrative events in the epic, while retaining traces of the features familiar in oral story telling. "Difficulties in explaining the use of the present disappear, when we adopt a broader view of the functions the present fulfils in the Latin tense system." Thomas' collection of his articles and notes, published between 1979 and 1998, treats the poet's transformation and renovation of his intertextual models, with emphasis on Callimachus. Thomas' response to R. K. Balot's article "Pindar, Virgil, and the Proem to Geòrgie 3," Phoenix 52 (1998) 83-94 occupies pages 9-10, footnote 13. Vergilian Bibliography, 1999-2000 147

raw_text: Gabriele Thome. "Vergil als alexandrinischer Dichter." Philologus 144 (2000) 90-115. Clark argues for the likelihood that Vergil took Aeacus' speech (Vat. gr. 2228, f. 482) and Euripidean function as model for Charon's speech ( Aen . 6.388-97) and created an innovative psychological dynamic for Charon. Faber brings the pseudo-Hesiodic Shield of Heracles into conjunction with Aeneas' shield. The allusions recall dreadful imagery unique to the shield of Heracles and so intensify the destructive power of Aeneas' armor, for not only the shield, but greaves, breastplate and sword are portrayed in language borrowed from Heracles' shield. 148 Alexander G. McKay

raw_text: Mary Frances Williams. "Seamus Heaney's Exposure and Vergil's Aeneid. " CML 19 (1999) 243-256. Todd's article sheds new light on the context of Enoch Powell's immigration speech in Birmingham, England in April 1968, "one of the most notorious usages of a classical quotation in 20th century British political life." Todd augments the observations of H. H. Huxley in Vergilius 44 (1998) 24-27. Williams studies the forty lines of Heaney's poem "Exposure" for reflections of essential elements of the Aeneid. Unlike Aeneas, the hero of Heaney's poem cannot see and recognize his comet, and so is unable to fulfill his destiny. 154 Alexander G. McKay

raw_text: Ross S. Kilpatrick. "Horatian Landscape in the Louvre's 'Concert Champêtre'?" Artibus et Historiae 41 (2000) 123- 131. Finley discusses and reproduces eleven pictures by Turner that are "either directly or indirectly concerned with the hero, Aeneas, and his exploits." Heuze studies Vergil "portraits" in the Sousse (Hadrumetum) mosaic and in Signorelli's "Dantesque" portrait in the marble intarsia flooring of the Duomo of Siena. Kilpatrick counters Christiane Joost-Gaugier' s 1999 interpretion of the Giorgione-Titian painting as evocative of Vergil's Arcadian melancholy. He detects Propertius in the elegant lute-player in red and Horace in the rustic figure gazed upon by Euterpe, with the Sabine farm in the distance.
